title: Part 14
text: far-off voices. A night bus. Anything. I once listened to thirty minutes of the inside of a Spanish cathedral which was fabu- lous. When you hear something like that you realise there’s ... It’s like there’s a music to whole world. The world is making music the whole time. It’s just that most of the time you’re too busy to notice.’ MOKY ‘Anyway I’d mentioned field recordings to my two previous therapists and each time they’d said, “Oh! You like recordings! Well, you’ ll really love these CDs I have of dolphins singing or whatever.” Laughs. ‘Don’t you like dolphins?’ Laughs. ‘No, of course | like dolphins. Dolphins are great. It’s just that it’s a bit obvious. A bit done to death. Anyway I told Val and he said exactly the same as the others. He said, “I have these recordings of waves. I think they’II help you relax.” And I thought OK. Fine. Whatever.’ Silence. ‘So I was sat in his office and I was talking about myself. Droning on and on. Boring myself rigid. And in the back- ground there was this recording of waves, and after a bit part of my brain started picking it apart, trying to understand it. And the more I listened, the more extraordinary it seemed. So I stopped talking and I asked him about it ‘And what did he say?’ ‘I remember he looked a bit startled. So I told him what I’d got from the recording — how it had been recorded in a cave — I could hear the reverberation — and the cave wasn’t smooth. Some of it — the roof or maybe the walls — had protuberances of some sort — I could hear water dripping from them. There was a lot of dripping so I thought perhaps the roof was covered with stalactites. I probably went on for quite a while about the recording because ... because, you know, it was so interesting. And Val just sat and listened and when I'd finished there was a longish pause and I thought he was going to say that I'd gone off topic and we had to get back to talking about me. But he didn’t. He talked about how I’d been completely different when I was talking about the recording, how my energy and my voice and my posture were completely different. How I was engaged in a way he’d never seen before. He said we’ve got an opportunity here. We can use this. He said he would send me a copy of the recording and I could listen to it at home and then next time we could talk about it again.’ Pause. ‘So that was all OK. That was fine. But before I left the strange thing happened. I asked him where the cave was. And he said he didn’t know, but he thought he could find out.’ ‘OK. He didn’t know where it was. Why was that strange?’ ‘It was strange because it was a lie. I didn’t know it at the time but a day or two later he sent me the recording and I sat down and listened to the whole thing straight through. There were two sessions. About five hours’ worth in total. At the end of the first session I heard the recordist fall down from wherever he was standing. He went into the water. I heard the splash and I heard him yelling and swearing. I recognised the voice. It was Val Ketterley.’ “You're certain?’ ‘Absolutely.’ “Why do you think he lied?’ ‘I’ve no idea. It was such an odd thing to lie about.’ Pause. ‘Anyway, as I say, I listened to the recording and I heard a lot more. For one thing bird cries. At certain periods the cave must have been full of birds — hundreds of them. And there was another thing that puzzled me. In some parts of the recording there was a sort of distant boom. I couldn’t make any sense of it at first. It was the sound of water hitting something solid and it was more or less regular. There was a rhythm to it. I thought it must be the waves hitting the walls in other caves and I real- ised that there wasn’t just one cave; there were several.’ Pause. ‘I could see it so clearly in my mind. The sequence of caves going on and on. The waves hitting the walls. The sea-birds flying. And then — I can’t explain it very well — I felt as if something had just lifted off me, some darkness, some pain. I felt sore, but I felt better. I felt ... I don’t know ... hopeful, I guess.’ ‘“That’s good.’ Pause. ‘So at our next session I told Val some of this. Actually I didn’t tell him very much of it. I didn’t trust him any more.’ ‘I don’t blame you.’ ‘And in a way I felt like I didn’t need him any more. That week listening to the sound of the caves changed things. I felt like the cave, like the recording itself — that was the thing that was going to heal me. I didn’t need the rest of it.’ Pause. ‘Anyway Val said that he’d been thinking about the record- ing and the way I'd reacted to it. And he asked if I’d like to go there and see it for myself. I asked where it was. He still wouldn’t say. He made a sort of speech about trust and how it was important for someone who'd been through what I'd been through to learn to trust people again. He said that this would be an exercise in trust. I was to come to his house and he would take me to the place where the recordings had been made and I could see the place for myself. He said there’d be a lot to explore.’ ‘What did you say?’ ‘I thought it was complete and utter crap. There was no way I was going anywhere with him. But I played along for a bit. I said I’d think about it. I kept going to our sessions for a couple more weeks, hoping he might let slip where the cave was. But he never did. In the end I stopped going’ ‘I don’t suppose you ever found out where the recording was made?’ ‘No, but I found a guy on a forum who specialises in record- ing water and I sent him the recording. Just on the off chance. He listened to it and he said something really strange. He didn’t think it was a cave at all. He thought that that booming sound I’d picked out meant it couldn’t be a cave. He said it had to be a building. A flooded cathedral was his guess. We did some research to try to find possible locations, but we didn’t get anywhere.’ Silence. ‘And Dr Ketterley didn’t say anything about a maze? A maze in a deserted garden?’ ‘Umm ... No. I don’t think so.’ ‘And he never mentioned a man called Matthew Rose Sorensen?’ ‘No.’ “You’re sure?’ “Yes. oa? = ol 2 7 = * ihanasbe a whe sti meee -- ae Pit = : wie elo re r mee: » igh oe * ae _= 2 f - 4 t t q 7 » oe i + Ao J a 4 . . 4 > ™ peer) Neel bow UNE @ R Susanna Clarke is the author of Jonathan Strange & Mr Norrell and The Ladies of Grace Adieu and Other Stories. This typeface is an adaptation of a style of letter that had been popularised for monumental work in stone by Eric Gill. Large scale drawings by Gill were given to Charles Malin, a Parisian punch-cutter, and his hand-cut punches were the basis for the font issued by Monotype. First used in a private translation called “The Passion of Perpetua and Felicity’, the italic was originally called Felicity. — bal) oY gi Le 1 ens te ent ; ee! It won the British Book Awards Newcomer of the Year, the Hugo Award and the World Fantasy Award in 2005. Susanna Clarke is also the author of the short story collection The Ladies of Grace Adieu. She lives in Derbyshire.
